sector: Utilities — Independent Power Producer (IPP) / merchant power + retail
bull_thesis:
Vistra is the most direct, lowest-multiple way to own the physical electricity that every dollar of AI infrastructure spending ultimately has to pay for. The company controls roughly 41 gigawatts of dispatchable and carbon-free nuclear generation concentrated in the two fastest-growing US power markets — ERCOT (Texas, 5-6% annual load growth through 2030) and PJM (Mid-Atlantic/Midwest, 2-3%) — an asset base that hyperscalers simply cannot replicate: the siting approvals, grid interconnection rights, and an operating nuclear license each take years and represent a near-unbuildable barrier to entry.

The AI-power story is no longer speculative; it is now contractual. A 20-year power-purchase agreement (PPA, a long-term contract to sell electricity at a fixed price) with Meta covering roughly 2,609 megawatts at Vistra's PJM nuclear plants, and a separate 20-year PPA with AWS covering roughly 1,200 megawatts at Comanche Peak in Texas, convert what was merchant optionality into investment-grade cash flow locked in through 2042.

First-quarter 2026 results confirmed the growth trajectory: ongoing adjusted EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortisation — the standard profitability measure for power companies) came in at $1.49 billion, up 20% year over year, on revenue of $5.64 billion, up 43%. The company's Eastern segment grew EBITDA 56%. Full-year 2026 guidance of $6.8-7.6 billion and 2027 guidance of $7.4-7.8 billion deliberately exclude the pending $4.7 billion Cogentrix gas acquisition (expected to close in the second half of 2026, adding roughly 5,500 megawatts), the already-closed Lotus deal (2,600 megawatts), and the economics of the Meta and AWS PPAs — a deliberate management understatement that should convert to upside as those deals are recognized.

Capital return has been relentless: the company plans to generate more than $10 billion in cash, is committed to returning at least $1.3 billion per year to shareholders, has a fresh $1 billion share-repurchase authorization, and has already reduced its share count by roughly 30% since 2021. A second credit-rating agency has upgraded Vistra to investment grade. Through all of this, the company trades at roughly 10 times forward EBITDA versus Constellation Energy at roughly 13 times and roughly 23 times forward earnings — a three-turn discount the market is gradually closing as investors reclassify Vistra from a cyclical merchant power company into a contracted-nuclear growth story. As the proportion of contracted megawatts rises, returns on invested capital and margins are increasing, not eroding, which means the competitive moat is widening at the same time the valuation gap is narrowing.
bear_thesis:
Much of what you are paying roughly $55 billion in market value plus roughly $19 billion of net debt to own is transient Texas scarcity-pricing dressed up as durable AI demand. The contracted PPAs with Meta and AWS (roughly 3,800 megawatts combined) are real, but they represent a minority of a roughly 41-gigawatt fleet. The majority of recent EBITDA comes from merchant Texas power — electricity sold at whatever the spot market pays on a given day — that reverts sharply when weather normalizes or scarcity conditions ease. The 2024-25 ERCOT price environment was a cyclical high-water mark, not a structural floor.

The headline profit figure is also less solid than it looks: roughly $723 million of the $1.03 billion in first-quarter 2026 GAAP net income is unrealized mark-to-market gains on hedging contracts (derivative contracts used to lock in future selling prices) — non-cash accounting entries that inflate the reported number without putting cash in the bank, or roughly 70% of the quarterly swing.

Leverage is high and rising. Total debt jumped from roughly $17 billion to roughly $19.2 billion in a single quarter as Vistra absorbed the Cogentrix and Lotus deals, against a thin equity base of only about $5.6 billion — a debt-to-equity ratio of roughly 330%. A sustained drop in power prices or a rise in credit spreads would squeeze both the share-buyback program and the investment-grade rating simultaneously.

The thesis also has a single-factor concentration problem: the entire investment case rests on AI data-centre power demand remaining durable, and so does the thesis for every other power-generation stock in the peer group. If a major hyperscaler trims its capital-expenditure (capex) outlook, Vistra de-rates alongside Constellation Energy and Talen Energy — there is no independent resolution driver.

Finally, the easy part of the re-rating is already behind us. The multiple has already moved from roughly 7 times to roughly 10 times EBITDA. The remaining gap to Constellation at 13 times closes slowly and is partly justified by Constellation's larger fully-contracted nuclear base. The 'sandbagged guidance' upside from Cogentrix and the PPAs is also at least partly reflected in analyst models. In a scenario where power prices normalize and AI enthusiasm cools, the stock could re-rate back toward the 7-8 times multiple appropriate for a merchant power company, implying meaningful downside.
sop_bull: $200-215 per share. Intrinsic value derived by applying a contracted-fleet earnings multiple to the whole company. Taking the 2027 guided adjusted EBITDA midpoint of roughly $7.6 billion, then adding estimated contributions from Cogentrix (roughly $0.5-0.7 billion) and the Meta/AWS PPA ramp (roughly $0.4-0.6 billion incremental) that are not yet in guidance, produces a run-rate EBITDA of roughly $8.5-8.8 billion. Applying 12.0 times (half a turn below Constellation's roughly 13 times, reflecting the still-rising contracted mix) yields an enterprise value of roughly $103 billion; subtracting roughly $20 billion of net debt and dividing by roughly 335 million diluted shares gives approximately $215 per share on the high case and approximately $200 at 11.5 times. The ongoing share-buyback (at least $1.3 billion per year, compounding the per-share math) adds further value over the holding period.
sop_bear: $110-125 per share. This is the power-price normalization case. Haircutting EBITDA to roughly $6.3-6.6 billion (reflecting ERCOT scarcity fading and no premium credited beyond the contracted floor) and applying an 8.5 times merchant-power multiple yields an enterprise value of roughly $56 billion; subtracting roughly $20 billion of net debt and dividing by roughly 335 million shares gives approximately $108 per share. At 9.0 times and crediting the contracted base, the floor rises to roughly $125. This is the anchor for an AI-pause plus power-price correction scenario.
sop_breakdown: Base case: roughly $7.8-8.0 billion forward run-rate ongoing adjusted EBITDA — the 2027 guidance midpoint of roughly $7.6 billion plus partial credit for Cogentrix and Lotus integration and the contracted PPA ramp, but deliberately not crediting the full sandbagged upside. Applying a blended 10.75 times enterprise-value-to-EBITDA multiple (a discount to Constellation's roughly 13 times reflecting the still-merchant-heavy mix and higher leverage, but a premium to the 8.5 times pure-merchant benchmark because roughly 3,800 megawatts of 20-year PPAs establish a contracted floor) produces an enterprise value of roughly $84-86 billion. Subtracting roughly $20 billion of net debt and dividing by roughly 335 million diluted shares gives roughly $165 per share. The ongoing buyback (reducing share count by roughly 1.5-2% per year) and the explicit guidance understatement on Cogentrix and the Meta/AWS economics provide the bridge from the current price toward and above fair value over 12-18 months; conversely, the remaining merchant exposure caps how far the multiple can re-rate without further contracting of the fleet.
risk_reward: Roughly balanced to slightly favorable, but not asymmetric. At the current price of roughly $164, the downside to the bear-case valuation of $110-125 is roughly 25-33%, while the upside to the base case of roughly $165 is essentially flat and the upside to the bull case of $200-215 is roughly 22-31%. That works out to a reward-to-risk ratio of roughly 1:1 across the spread between the bear and bull scenarios, with the base case sitting almost exactly at today's price. The favorable tilt comes from the guidance understatement — Cogentrix and the PPAs converting to hard reported numbers — rather than from a discounted entry price. The cheap-merchant re-rating that drove the stock from 7 to 10 times EBITDA is largely captured.
peer_comps_note:
The true peer group is the AI-power and nuclear-merchant independent power-producer cohort: Constellation Energy (CEG), Talen Energy (TLN), and NRG Energy. Live multiples as of 2026-06-22: Constellation at a market cap of roughly $88.7 billion, roughly 23.6 times forward earnings, and roughly 13 times forward enterprise-value-to-EBITDA — the largest US power producer following its $29.1 billion Calpine acquisition in January 2026, with the most fully contracted nuclear fleet, and the premium benchmark for the group. Vistra at roughly $55.2 billion market cap, roughly 14.7 times forward earnings on a next-twelve-months basis, and roughly 10 times forward EBITDA — the value name of the cohort, cheapest on both metrics, with 2026 EBITDA guided to $6.8-7.6 billion and 2027 to $7.4-7.8 billion. Talen Energy at roughly 11.5 times EBITDA on 2026 guidance of $1.75-2.05 billion (up roughly 43%) — the highest-beta option, smaller at roughly 13 gigawatts, with Susquehanna nuclear capacity and a roughly 1,920-megawatt Amazon power contract; the merchant portion of this cohort de-rates toward roughly 8 times during pullbacks.

Ranking within the group: on valuation, Vistra is cheapest (10 times) then Talen (11.5 times) then Constellation (13 times). On growth, Talen is fastest (+43% EBITDA) versus Vistra and Constellation in the mid-teens to 20% range, boosted by mergers and acquisitions. On margin quality, Constellation is highest (fully contracted nuclear), Vistra is mid-range (contracted plus merchant mix), and Talen is most volatile (merchant-heavy). On returns and scale, Constellation leads, then Vistra, then Talen. All three benefit directly from AI-capex-driven power demand; Constellation (nuclear-pure) and Talen (Susquehanna co-location) are the cleaner nuclear plays, while Vistra is the more diversified ERCOT-plus-PJM platform with the most dispatchable gas optionality via Cogentrix. Vistra is the discounted, more diversified, and more levered member of a peer group that has all appreciated sharply on the same AI-power factor.
role_in_scaleout: Vistra is a first-tier power-generation supplier — it owns the actual electrons (gas plus carbon-free nuclear, roughly 41 gigawatts) in ERCOT and PJM that hyperscaler and sovereign-AI computing facilities must procure before any chip, optical component, or transformer comes into play. Demand durability is high for the contracted roughly 3,800 megawatts (20-year PPAs with Meta and AWS) and merely cyclical for the merchant remainder, with the structural ERCOT and PJM load-growth rates (5-6% and 2-3% respectively through 2030) providing a floor for the uncontracted portion.
consensus_delta:
Where we disagree with the market: the street's residual error is the legacy one — pricing Vistra as an over-levered Texas merchant power company that deserves a 7-9 times EBITDA multiple and whose ERCOT earnings will mean-revert. That mispricing is roughly 60% corrected: the stock has re-rated from roughly 7 times to roughly 10 times, and the AI-power growth narrative is now widely accepted. What the market still underweights is twofold. First, Vistra's 2026 and 2027 guidance explicitly excludes Cogentrix (roughly 5,500 megawatts, $4.7 billion deal), the already-closed Lotus deal, and the Meta/AWS PPA economics — so consensus EBITDA estimates for both years understate the contracted run-rate, a sandbagged number that should print as upside when those contributions are recognized. Second, as the contracted-megawatt proportion rises, the appropriate multiple should converge toward Constellation's roughly 13 times rather than staying three turns below it.

The opposite error also exists, however, and partially offsets the first: many current holders treat Vistra as a pure secular AI-power compounder and under-discount the fact that the majority of EBITDA is still merchant electricity — genuinely price-sensitive revenue that does not carry a 13-times multiple. Net: the easy opportunity (buying a deeply discounted merchant stock) is largely spent; the remaining edge is the guidance understatement converting to hard prints, which is real but modest and at least partly already modeled by analysts.
valley_of_death: Three risks that could each last three to nine months. First, an ERCOT or PJM power-price correction: a mild summer or the natural fade of scarcity-pricing conditions would compress merchant EBITDA and undermine the 'AI demand equals durable cash flow' narrative well before the PPAs ramp — the single biggest near-term de-rating risk. Second, an AI capital-expenditure scare: AI-capex spending accounts for roughly 40% of 2026 S&P 500 earnings-per-share growth and is the dominant shared factor running under the entire power-generation trade. Any hyperscaler capex guide-down would de-rate the whole power-generation group simultaneously, and Vistra has zero independent resolution driver separate from Constellation Energy and Talen Energy in this scenario. Third, balance-sheet and financing risk: debt rose from roughly $17 billion to roughly $19.2 billion in a single quarter as Vistra absorbed Cogentrix and Lotus; the $4.7 billion Cogentrix close in the second half of 2026 adds more. In a higher-for-longer interest-rate or wider credit-spread environment, refinancing costs and the buyback compete for the same cash. None of these risks represents a cash-burn crisis — Vistra converts more than 60% of EBITDA to free cash flow — but a simultaneous power-price correction and AI-pause combination is a credible path to a 25-33% drawdown toward the bear-case valuation.
positioning_washout: Mechanical forced-selling pressure is limited. Vistra is not held primarily for its dividend (which is small); the holder base is drawn by buyback-driven per-share growth and AI-power momentum. There is therefore no yield-investor liquidation trigger of the kind that hits high-dividend utilities when rates rise. The risk runs the other way: Vistra has become a crowded momentum and thematic-AI position held across generalist and sector funds. A de-grossing event — an AI-capex scare or a weak ERCOT-price quarter — would cause fast-money to trim the entire power-generation cohort (Constellation, Vistra, Talen) together, producing correlated selling pressure rather than a clean stock-specific washout. There is no forced-seller event on the horizon that would create an asymmetric entry; the better entry comes from a fundamental flush such as a soft ERCOT-price print or an AI-capex headline, which is exactly the 'wait for the wash-out' scenario the overall verdict calls for.
forcing_function: No single hard binary event; instead, a sequence of soft, extended milestones. First, the Cogentrix close in the second half of 2026 will convert roughly $0.5-0.7 billion of currently excluded EBITDA into guidance, forcing consensus estimates higher. Second, the second- and third-quarter 2026 earnings releases (roughly August and November 2026) are the real test: they will show whether ERCOT summer scarcity pricing holds the merchant portion of EBITDA, and they are the first quarters in which PPA ramp contributions will be visible. Third, the commencement of Meta/AWS PPA revenues and any new hyperscaler contract announcement each pull the valuation multiple toward Constellation's fully-contracted benchmark. None of these is a hard, contractual catalyst with capped asymmetry; all are operationally driven milestones that can slip or surprise in either direction.
moderator_conclusion:
Vistra is the highest-quality, lowest-multiple member of the power-generation peer group and a genuine first-tier supplier of the one input — physical electricity — that gates the entire AI build-out. The forensic review found management credibility 4 of 5; trajectory strengthening; moat narrow but real and widening; secular threat manageable. That assessment is well-supported: EBITDA up 20%, real 20-year contracts with Meta and AWS, a second investment-grade upgrade, a disciplined buyback, and guidance that deliberately excludes the two largest growth drivers.

However, the regime framework forces a down-rating from what the fundamentals alone would justify. These are not hard-dated catalysts inside a favorable entry window — they are soft, extended operational milestones (Cogentrix close, quarterly ERCOT earnings prints, PPA ramp), and the central catalyst (the AI-power-demand re-rating) has already substantially fired: the multiple has expanded from roughly 7 times to roughly 10 times EBITDA. The framework explicitly penalizes already-fired catalysts and prizes resolution-driver independence; Vistra's entire thesis depends on the same single shared macro factor — AI-capex durability — as every other stock in the basket, with no independent driver.

The base-case valuation of roughly $165 per share sits essentially at today's price of roughly $164. Most of the cheap-merchant re-rating is captured, the reward-to-risk ratio is roughly 1:1, and the remaining edge from the guidance understatement is real but modest and at least partly modeled by the street.

The verdict is 'wait for the wash-out,' conviction 3: the quality is real and worth owning, but the right entry is a specific fundamental flush — a weak ERCOT-price quarter or an AI-capex headline that de-rates the cohort — to obtain an entry toward the $120-140 range rather than at fair value today. The value conviction (stripping away the catalyst and regime overlay) is also 3: the valuation is roughly fair at the base-case, the moat is widening so no value-trap discount applies, and the forensic quality is high — a fairly-priced quality compounder, not a margin-of-safety bargain. Vistra's role in the scale-out analysis: it sells the electrons every AI dollar must pay for; demand is durable for the contracted roughly 3,800 megawatts and cyclical for the merchant remainder.
interrogator_dossier:
Vistra Corp. (VST) — Forensic Dossier

Source basis: VST first-quarter 2026 earnings release (8-K filed 2026-05-07), first-quarter 2026 earnings-call transcript, third- and fourth-quarter 2025 results, 10-Q filed for fiscal year 2026, and live market data as of 2026-06-22.

Narrative arc

Vistra entered this period as a deeply discounted, over-levered Texas merchant power company whose story was straightforward: aggressive buybacks would reduce the share count faster than the market noticed. That story has since expanded and re-rated into a national AI-power-demand growth narrative without abandoning the capital-returns spine. From 2024 into early 2025, Vistra executed relentless buybacks (share count down roughly 30% since 2021), bolted on the Energy Harbor nuclear acquisition in 2024 (adding roughly 6.4 gigawatts of carbon-free capacity), and locked in near-term earnings through comprehensive hedging. From mid-2025 to the present, management pivoted to a structural load-growth framing — 5-6% annual ERCOT growth through 2030, 2-3% in PJM — described not as a forecast but as already visible in interconnection queues and physical load connections. New growth pillars added: a 20-year PPA with Meta for roughly 2,609 megawatts at PJM nuclear plants; a 20-year PPA with AWS for roughly 1,200 megawatts at Comanche Peak in Texas; the pending $4.7 billion Cogentrix acquisition (roughly 5,500 megawatts of gas generation, expected second half of 2026); the closed Lotus deal (roughly 2,600 megawatts, November 2025); and roughly 4,500 megawatts of organic gas development. The buyback emphasis broadened from 'shrink the count' to 'fund growth and return at least $1.3 billion per year.' This is a genuine thesis expansion — from cyclical merchant to contracted AI-growth — corroborated by hard contracts.

Claims versus financial statements

Roughly 20% EBITDA growth and structural demand: first-quarter 2026 ongoing adjusted EBITDA of $1.49 billion versus roughly $1.25 billion in the prior year (+20%); revenue $5.64 billion versus $3.93 billion (+43%); Eastern segment EBITDA +56%, Texas +19% — verified.

Swing to profitability: GAAP net income of +$1.03 billion versus -$317 million in the prior year, but roughly $723 million of that swing is unrealized mark-to-market gains on hedging contracts — roughly 70% of the move is non-cash — verified but flagged.

2026 EBITDA guidance of $6.8-7.6 billion and 2027 of $7.4-7.8 billion: the first-quarter run-rate supports the midpoint; guidance explicitly excludes Cogentrix and the Meta/AWS PPA economics — credible and deliberately understated.

More than $10 billion of cash generation and at least $1.3 billion returned annually: year-to-date buybacks of roughly $525 million and roughly $600 million of total returns in four months — verified.

Investment-grade balance sheet: upgraded at a second rating agency, but total debt rose from roughly $17 billion to roughly $19.2 billion in a single quarter and equity stands at only roughly $5.6 billion (debt-to-equity roughly 330%); net leverage of roughly 2.5-2.7 times EBITDA is within investment-grade territory for a contracted power company — mixed picture, worth monitoring.

Tone and confidence trajectory

Rising. Language in 2025 and into first-quarter 2026 shifted from defensive and hedging ('locked in,' 'comprehensive hedge') to offensive and demand-forward ('structural load growth,' 'not speculative,' 'interconnection queues'). Management volunteers multi-year load specifics, raised the 2027 EBITDA opportunity, and added a fresh $1 billion buyback authorization. There is no shift toward cost-efficiency or defensive framing.

Guidance credibility

High. Vistra has a multi-year track record of meeting or beating EBITDA guidance and over-delivering on buybacks (share count down more than 30% since 2021). A soft fourth-quarter 2025 print briefly pressured the stock, but first-quarter 2026 results (+20%) revalidated the trajectory and management reaffirmed 2026 guidance. Excluding Cogentrix and the PPAs has historically resolved to upside. Overall reliability: high, with one fourth-quarter 2025 wobble as the lone exception.

Analyst pressure points

Three recurring themes in analyst questions: the gap between headline load-growth announcements and actually contracted megawatts; the rising leverage and M&A appetite at roughly $19 billion of debt; and how much EBITDA is transient ERCOT scarcity pricing versus durable structural demand. Management consistently deflects toward the structural-demand frame rather than modelling a power-price mean-reversion scenario.

Key positives and concerns

Positives: real, dated 20-year contracts with Meta (roughly 2,609 megawatts) and AWS (roughly 1,200 megawatts) convert the narrative to bankable cash flow; capital-return discipline through the re-rating; investment-grade upgrade at a second agency; guidance excludes the two biggest growth drivers.

Concerns: roughly 70% of the first-quarter GAAP swing is non-cash hedge mark-to-market; absolute debt is rising ($17 billion to $19.2 billion) against a thin equity base; the ERCOT scarcity tailwind is partly cyclical; and the investment thesis shares its single macro driver — AI-capex durability — with the entire peer group, creating meaningful correlation risk.

Hidden signals

Terminology has deliberately shifted from 'merchant/hedged' to 'contracted/carbon-free/structural' — management is intentionally repositioning the stock out of the cyclical power-company bucket toward the Constellation Energy-style contracted-nuclear bucket, which trades at roughly three times the multiple. Capital-allocation language has broadened from 'buyback' to 'fund growth and return.' The Meta deal at PJM (not Texas) consciously diversifies AI-demand exposure away from ERCOT.

Capital-allocation verdict

Strengthening. The single most important question is how much EBITDA is durable contracted cash flow versus transient ERCOT scarcity pricing — the re-rating assumes the AI premium is permanent, but a meaningful share of trailing EBITDA is merchant Texas power that will mean-revert; the contracted PPAs (roughly 3,800 megawatts) de-risk the forward base but are not yet a majority of the fleet. Competitive moat: scale cost advantages, regulatory licensing, and asset scarcity — roughly 41 gigawatts of dispatchable and nuclear generation in ERCOT and PJM that hyperscalers cannot replicate (siting approvals, interconnection rights, and a nuclear operating license each take years). The moat is narrow but real and widening, capped at narrow by the merchant tail and leverage. Secular backdrop: the dominant vector is a tailwind (electrification plus AI-power equals an energy-transition beneficiary); the only material risks are an AI-capex stall (manageable, as load growth also rests on electrification and reindustrialisation) and power-price normalization (cyclical, not secular). The discount versus Constellation reflects investor skepticism, not a shrinking business.

Management credibility 4 of 5; trajectory strengthening; moat narrow but real; moat trend widening; secular threat manageable.
skeptic_kill_fact: No disqualifying fact was found. Every load-bearing figure checks out against primary sources: first-quarter 2026 ongoing adjusted EBITDA of $1.49 billion and revenue of $5.64 billion (8-K filed 2026-05-07); the debt increase from roughly $17.0 billion to roughly $19.2 billion and equity of roughly $5.6 billion implying roughly 330% debt-to-equity (10-Q, fiscal 2026); the pending $4.7 billion Cogentrix acquisition of roughly 5,500 megawatts explicitly excluded from reaffirmed 2026 guidance of $6.8-7.6 billion; and the Meta roughly 2,609-megawatt and AWS 1,200-megawatt 20-year nuclear PPAs (SEC 8-K filings). The bear arguments — the merchant tail, rising leverage, and the basket-wide single-factor AI-capex risk — are all real but are already priced into the 'wait for the wash-out,' conviction-3 down-rating, not into a disqualifying flaw.
skeptic_corrections: One material factual error was found and corrected. The bull thesis and forensic dossier both assert that Vistra is up roughly 22% year over year and that 'the AI re-rating has already substantially fired.' This is incorrect according to live market data: Vistra is down roughly 7.5% over the trailing year, trading at $163.75 against a 52-week high of $219.82 — roughly 25% below its peak. The error favors the thesis rather than undermining it: the moderator's central objection — 'the name has already run, the re-rating is spent, the reward-to-risk ratio is 1:1' — is materially weaker than stated, because the stock has in fact pulled back roughly 25% from its highs, which is closer to the 'wait for the capitulation flush' scenario the verdict recommends waiting for. The 'already-priced-in' demotion rests partly on a false premise; the genuine entry opportunity is better than the dossier implies. A second, minor correction: the first-quarter GAAP net income of roughly $1.03 billion with roughly $723 million (70%) attributed to non-cash hedge mark-to-market gains — the headline figure is verified and the roughly 70% non-cash characterization is directionally fair; the durable-versus-transient EBITDA caveat stands.
skeptic_evidence: Vistra Q1 2026 8-K earnings release (filed 2026-05-07, SEC EDGAR 000169281926000011): Ongoing Operations Adjusted EBITDA $1,494M, operating revenue $5.64B vs $3.93B PY; 2026 guidance reaffirmed $6.8-7.6B, explicitly excludes Cogentrix.

Vistra Q1 2026 10-Q (SEC EDGAR 000169281926000014, FY2026): long-term debt incl. current maturities $19,163M (up from $17,043M); total equity $5,610M (D/E ~330%); Lotus acquisition closed at $1,231M; $2.25B senior secured notes (Jan-2026) + $4.0B senior unsecured notes (Apr-2026) issued.

Vistra SEC 8-Ks / World Nuclear News (2025-2026): 20-year PPAs -- Meta ~2,609 MW across PJM nuclear (incl. 433 MW uprates), AWS 1,200 MW at Comanche Peak (delivery from Q4-2027, full ramp by 2032); ~3,800 MW contracted total.

Live market data (as of 2026-06-18): VST $163.75, market cap ~$55.2B, 52-week high $219.82, trailing-12-month return ~ -7.5% -- i.e. the stock is ~25% off its high and DOWN YoY, contradicting the dossier's '+22% YoY / re-rate already fired' claim.

scale.role: Cheapest way to own the dispatchable+nuclear power every AI dollar pays for; ~41 GW in the two highest-load-growth US markets, ~3,800 MW of 20-yr nuclear PPAs (Meta/AWS).
scale.rationale: The single best risk/reward in the whole 45-name complex and a CORE franchise (~86): firm dispatchable + nuclear under 20-yr hyperscaler PPAs in the highest-load-growth US markets, plus the Cogentrix optionality excluded from guidance. Skeptic verdict CONFIRMED_WITH_CORRECTIONS, and the correction cuts FOR the thesis — the bull's '+22% YoY / re-rate already fired' premise was FALSE; VST is actually DOWN ~7.5% YoY, ~25% off its high, i.e. the re-rate has NOT fired here — so only a ~4pt haircut. Base ~$165 ~at spot, bull +22-31%; the merchant tail and ~330% D/E are the offsets (in valuation_posture, not the conviction). This is the CORE I would seat at full weight today — enter_now_carry. [Honesty note: at ~$164 VST is FAIR to its own $160-170 SoP (+0.8%), NOT cheap on the absolute number — the 'cheap'/priority read is RELATIVE (cheapest of the power-gen cluster, ~26% off its 52wk high, below the 200-DMA), i.e. a drawdown-staging entry, not an absolute-SoP discount.]
scale.demand_durability: Very high — firm dispatchable + nuclear power under 20-yr hyperscaler PPAs in load-growth markets; merchant tail and ~330% D/E are the offsets.
scale.valuation_posture: fair vs SoP ($160-170, +0.8%); cheap only relative (-26% off 52wk high, <200-DMA)
